title: PySpark for Data Engineers: Junior-Level Track
description: Build PySpark capability from the ground up: distributed computing fundamentals, SparkSession, DataFrames, transformations, actions, reading and writing data, and production pipeline patterns.

chapters.title: Why Spark Exists
chapters.description: Understand why distributed computing is necessary, how Spark solves the single-machine bottleneck, and how the NordGrid Energy scenario maps to a real-world data engineering context.

blocks.content: Every data pipeline starts on one machine. You write a Python script, it reads a file, transforms the data, and writes the result. For thousands of rows this works perfectly — the script finishes in seconds, memory usage barely registers, and the disk handles the read-write cycle without complaint. But data grows. NordGrid Energy, a Northern European grid operator, started with 10,000 smart meters reporting daily energy consumption. Five years later, they manage 500,000 meters. The daily readings file grew from 2 megabytes to 1.5 gigabytes. The Python script that once finished in 4 seconds now takes 35 minutes. This lesson is about why that slowdown happens and what it tells us about the limits of a single computer.
The Three Walls

A computer has three physical resources that determine how fast it can process data: the disk (where data is stored), memory (where data is held while being worked on), and the CPU (where computations happen). Each of these resources has a maximum throughput — a speed limit that no software optimization can exceed. When your data grows large enough to hit any one of these limits, your script slows down dramatically. These limits are sometimes called the three walls of single-machine processing, and understanding them is the first step toward understanding why distributed computing exists.

blocks.title: The three physical limits of a single machine
blocks.content:
Data on Disk                Memory (RAM)               CPU
┌──────────────────────┐  ┌────────────────────┐  ┌────────────────────┐
│  1.5 GB daily file   │  │  Must fit in RAM   │  │  Processes rows     │
│                      │  │  to be processed   │  │  one at a time      │
│  Read speed:          │  │                    │  │  (per core)         │
│  ~500 MB/s (SSD)     │  │  Typical server:   │  │                    │
│  ~100 MB/s (HDD)     │  │  16–64 GB          │  │  Typical server:    │
│                      │  │                    │  │  4–16 cores         │
└──────────────────────┘  └────────────────────┘  └────────────────────┘
        │                         │                         │
     Wall 1:                  Wall 2:                  Wall 3:
     Disk I/O                 Memory                   Compute
     bottleneck               capacity                 throughput
blocks.caption: Every single machine hits one of these three limits as data grows. The first wall you hit depends on the task — but eventually, all three become constraints.

blocks.content: Wall 1 — Disk Throughput

The first wall is usually the disk. Before your script can transform any data, it must read that data from the hard drive or SSD into memory. A modern SSD reads at roughly 500 megabytes per second. That sounds fast, but NordGrid’s 1.5 GB daily file takes 3 seconds just to load into memory — before any processing begins. When NordGrid runs a monthly summary that reads 30 days of files, that is 45 GB of data. At 500 MB/s, reading alone takes 90 seconds. On an older spinning hard drive at 100 MB/s, it takes 7.5 minutes. Writing results back to disk doubles the I/O time. For a simple read-transform-write pipeline, the disk often accounts for more than half the total runtime.

Think of the disk as a warehouse door. No matter how many workers you have inside the warehouse, goods can only enter and leave through that one door at a fixed rate. A wider door (a faster SSD) helps, but there is a physical limit to how wide one door can be. When data exceeds what the door can handle in a reasonable time, the only option is to have multiple doors — multiple machines, each with its own disk, reading their own portion of the data simultaneously.
Wall 2 — Memory Capacity

The second wall is memory. When your Python script reads a CSV file into a Pandas DataFrame, the entire dataset must fit in RAM. A 1.5 GB CSV file typically expands to 3–5 GB in memory because Python objects carry overhead — each string, each number, each row structure occupies more memory than the raw bytes on disk. A server with 16 GB of RAM can hold one day’s NordGrid data comfortably. But a monthly analysis (45 GB on disk, 90–150 GB in memory) is physically impossible on that machine. The data simply does not fit. Your script crashes with a MemoryError, and no amount of code optimization can fix a problem that is fundamentally about physical capacity.

blocks.title: Why bigger hardware has limits
blocks.content: You can buy a server with 256 GB or even 1 TB of RAM. But bigger hardware is expensive, has diminishing returns, and eventually hits its own ceiling. A single machine with 1 TB of RAM still cannot process 2 TB of data in memory. And that 1 TB machine costs 10x more than ten 128 GB machines combined. This economic reality is one of the reasons distributed computing exists.
Wall 3 — Compute Throughput

The third wall is the CPU. Even if the data fits in memory and the disk is fast enough, the CPU must process every row. A single CPU core can typically evaluate a few hundred million simple operations per second. For NordGrid’s 500,000 daily readings, that is plenty — a single core handles the computation in milliseconds. But consider the annual reprocessing job: 500,000 meters × 365 days = 182.5 million rows. Add a complex transformation — parsing dates, computing rolling averages, joining with a reference table — and each row requires hundreds of operations. The total compute time stretches to minutes, then tens of minutes, as the data grows.

Modern servers have multiple cores (4, 8, 16, or more), and well-written programs can use them in parallel. But writing correct multi-threaded code in Python is difficult and error-prone, and Python’s Global Interpreter Lock limits true parallelism for many operations. Even without Python’s limitations, a single server’s core count is fixed. When the compute requirement exceeds what 16 cores can deliver, you need more cores — which means more machines.
NordGrid’s Growth Trajectory

blocks.title: How NordGrid’s data growth hit the single-machine walls
blocks.content:
Year 1       Year 2       Year 3       Year 4       Year 5
10K meters   50K meters   150K meters  300K meters  500K meters
2 MB/day     15 MB/day    200 MB/day   800 MB/day   1.5 GB/day

█              ██            █████         █████████     ██████████████

4 seconds    20 seconds   3 minutes    12 minutes   35 minutes
Python OK    Python OK    Python slow  Python pain  Python limit
                                                    ─────────────
                                                    ▲ Something
                                                      must change
blocks.caption: NordGrid’s data volume grew 750x over five years. The single-machine Python pipeline that worked perfectly at 10,000 meters became unsustainable at 500,000. The data outgrew the machine.

blocks.content: NordGrid’s story is not unusual. Every organization that collects data at scale eventually hits the same walls. The data grows gradually — 5% more meters this quarter, a new sensor type next quarter, a regulatory requirement to retain five years of history instead of two. Each increment is manageable on its own. But the compound effect is exponential growth that eventually overwhelms any single machine. The question is not whether you will hit the walls, but when. Distributed computing is the answer to that question, and Apache Spark is the tool that makes it practical. In the next lesson, you will see how the simple idea of using multiple machines together transforms this problem completely.

blocks.title: Common misconception
blocks.content: The bottleneck is not Python being slow. Python is slower than Java or C++, but the fundamental limits are physical: disk read speed, memory capacity, and core count. Rewriting NordGrid’s pipeline in a faster language would help, but it would not change the fact that one machine cannot read 45 GB of data faster than its disk allows, or hold 150 GB of data in 16 GB of RAM.

The previous lesson described three walls that every single machine eventually hits: disk throughput, memory capacity, and compute throughput. Each wall is a physical limit — you cannot read data faster than the disk allows, hold more data than RAM permits, or compute faster than the cores can execute. But what if you used more than one machine? If one disk can read 500 MB/s, two disks on two machines can read 1 GB/s. If one machine has 16 GB of RAM, four machines have 64 GB. If one server has 8 cores, ten servers have 80. This is the cluster idea: instead of building one impossibly powerful machine, connect many ordinary machines and divide the work among them.
The Control Room Analogy

NordGrid’s operations center in Stavanger manages maintenance across four regions: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, and WEST. Imagine the shift supervisor receives a list of 200 turbines that need inspection today. If only one field crew is available, that crew must drive to every turbine, inspect it, record the results, and move to the next. Even working at full speed, one crew cannot finish 200 inspections in a single shift. But the supervisor has four field crews, one stationed in each region. The supervisor splits the list: 50 turbines per crew, grouped by geography. Each crew inspects its 50 turbines simultaneously. The work that would take one crew four days finishes in one day.

The supervisor did not make any single crew faster. Each crew still inspects turbines at the same pace. What changed is that the work is divided and performed in parallel. The supervisor’s job is coordination: splitting the list, assigning crews, and collecting results. The crews’ job is execution: doing the actual inspections. This division — one coordinator, many workers — is exactly how a computer cluster operates. The coordinator is called the driver. The workers are called executors. You will learn more about these roles in lesson 4.

blocks.title: One crew vs four crews working in parallel
blocks.caption: Parallel processing does not make any individual worker faster. It makes the overall job faster by dividing the work across multiple workers who execute simultaneously.

blocks.content: Scaling Out vs Scaling Up

There are two ways to handle growing data. Scaling up means buying a bigger machine: more RAM, faster disks, more CPU cores. Scaling up works, but it has diminishing returns and hard limits. A server with 1 TB of RAM costs dramatically more than a server with 128 GB, and at some point the largest machine money can buy still is not enough. Scaling out means adding more machines. Instead of one powerful server, you use ten ordinary servers connected by a network. The total capacity of the cluster — total RAM, total disk throughput, total cores — is the sum of all the machines. Need more capacity? Add another machine.

blocks.title: Scaling out is not free
blocks.content: A cluster introduces complexity that a single machine does not have: network latency between machines, the possibility that one machine fails during processing, and the need for software that coordinates the work. The engineering effort to manage a cluster is real. The reason organizations accept this complexity is that the alternative — waiting 35 minutes for a pipeline that needs to finish in 5 — is worse.

left.label: Scaling Up (bigger machine)
points: Buy a server with 512 GB RAM

right.label: Scaling Out (more machines)
points: Buy 8 servers with 64 GB RAM each (512 GB total)
8 disks read in parallel: 4,000 MB/s total
No practical limit: keep adding machines

blocks.content: NordGrid’s data engineering team chose to scale out. Their existing servers each had 64 GB of RAM and 8 CPU cores — perfectly adequate machines, but not powerful enough individually for the growing workload. Instead of replacing them with one expensive machine, they connected eight of these servers into a cluster. The combined cluster has 512 GB of RAM, 64 CPU cores, and 8 separate disks that can all read data simultaneously. The 35-minute pipeline now finishes in under 5 minutes — not because any single machine is faster, but because eight machines share the work.
What a Cluster Needs

A cluster is not just a collection of machines sitting in the same room. For multiple machines to work together on the same problem, three things must happen. First, the data must be divided into pieces small enough for each machine to handle — this is called partitioning, and you will learn about it in detail in lesson 5. Second, each machine must know which piece to work on and what to do with it — this requires a coordinator that plans the work and assigns it to workers. Third, the results from each machine must be combined into a single final answer — the coordinator must collect partial results and merge them.

These three steps — divide the data, execute in parallel, combine the results — are the fundamental pattern of distributed computing. Every distributed system, from web search engines to weather simulations to NordGrid’s meter processing pipeline, follows this pattern. The challenge is not the idea (which is simple) but the engineering: handling machine failures, managing network communication, ensuring that partial results combine correctly, and making all of this invisible to the person writing the code. This is exactly the problem that Apache Spark solves, and it is the subject of the next lesson.

blocks.title: Key takeaway
blocks.content: A cluster solves the single-machine bottleneck by dividing work across many machines. No individual machine is faster — but the combined effort finishes the job in a fraction of the time. The pattern is always the same: divide the data, execute in parallel, combine the results.

The previous lesson described how a cluster of machines can process data faster by dividing work and executing in parallel. But a cluster is just hardware — machines connected by a network. To actually use a cluster, you need software that knows how to split your data, send pieces to different machines, run your transformations on each piece, handle failures, and combine results into a single output. Writing that software from scratch for every pipeline would be enormously complex. Apache Spark is a distributed data processing engine that handles all of this for you. You write your data transformations in familiar Python code, and Spark takes care of distributing the work across however many machines are available.
The MapReduce Era

Spark was not the first tool to solve this problem. In 2004, Google published a paper describing a system called MapReduce, which divided distributed computation into two phases: a map phase that processed data in parallel, and a reduce phase that combined results. Hadoop, an open-source implementation of MapReduce, became the dominant framework for processing large datasets through the late 2000s and early 2010s. Hadoop worked, but it had a significant limitation: after every map and reduce phase, it wrote all intermediate results to disk. For a pipeline with five transformation steps, Hadoop read from disk and wrote to disk five times. Each disk round-trip took minutes, so even simple pipelines were slow.

In 2009, researchers at UC Berkeley’s AMPLab began developing a new approach. Their key insight was that most data processing pipelines apply a series of transformations to the same data — filter it, transform it, aggregate it, join it with another dataset. If the intermediate results could stay in memory instead of being written to disk between steps, the pipeline would be dramatically faster. They called this system Spark, and when they benchmarked it against Hadoop MapReduce, certain workloads ran 10 to 100 times faster. Spark became an Apache project in 2013 and is now the most widely used distributed data processing engine in the industry.

blocks.title: Hadoop MapReduce vs Spark — disk round-trips
blocks.content:
Hadoop MapReduce (disk between every step):

Disk → [Step 1] → Disk → [Step 2] → Disk → [Step 3] → Disk → [Step 4] → Disk
  ↑      read       ↑       read      ↑      read       ↑      read       ↑
 write              write             write              write             write

Spark (in-memory between steps):

Disk → [Step 1] → Memory → [Step 2] → Memory → [Step 3] → Memory → [Step 4] → Disk
  ↑      read                                                              ↑
 one disk read                                                        one disk write
blocks.caption: Spark keeps intermediate data in memory, eliminating the disk writes between steps that made Hadoop slow. The data is read from disk once at the start and written once at the end.

blocks.content: What Spark Is Not

It is worth clarifying what Spark does not do, because new learners often confuse it with other tools. Spark is not a database — it does not store data permanently or respond to queries from multiple users simultaneously. Spark is not a storage system — it reads from and writes to external storage (files, databases, cloud storage) but does not manage where files live. Spark is not an orchestration tool — it does not schedule pipelines to run at specific times or manage dependencies between jobs. Tools like Apache Airflow handle orchestration; tools like Delta Lake handle storage; Spark handles the processing in between.
What Spark Does for You

Spark is not a database, not a storage system, and not a programming language. It is a processing engine — software that takes your data transformations, distributes them across a cluster, and returns the results. When you write a Spark pipeline, you are not writing network code, thread management, failure recovery logic, or data distribution algorithms. You are writing the same kinds of operations you would write in Pandas — filter rows, add columns, group and aggregate, join tables — and Spark handles everything else. The value proposition is simple: you think about your data logic, and Spark thinks about the distribution.

rows: Sends the filter to all machines; each machine filters its own portion
Group by region and compute average
Moves matching rows to the same machine, then aggregates
Distributes both datasets by the join key so matches land together
Each machine writes its portion; results appear as one logical output

blocks.content: The Spark Ecosystem

Spark started as a data processing engine, but it has grown into an ecosystem of libraries built on the same core. The core engine handles distributed data processing. Spark SQL adds the ability to query data using SQL syntax alongside the Python API. Structured Streaming extends Spark to process data that arrives continuously (like real-time sensor readings) rather than in daily batches. MLlib provides machine learning algorithms that run on the cluster. GraphX handles graph-structured data. For NordGrid’s data engineering work — and for this course — the core engine and Spark SQL are the most important components. You will encounter both throughout the remaining modules.

blocks.title: Why Spark won
blocks.content: Spark replaced Hadoop MapReduce not because it introduced a fundamentally new idea, but because it removed an unnecessary bottleneck: writing intermediate results to disk. Keeping data in memory between steps turned hour-long pipelines into minute-long pipelines. The lesson for data engineers is that the biggest performance wins often come from eliminating unnecessary data movement, not from faster algorithms.

Spark is open source, maintained by the Apache Software Foundation, and backed by Databricks (the company founded by Spark’s original creators). It runs on all major cloud providers — AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud — as well as on-premises clusters. NordGrid runs Spark on a managed cloud service, which means they do not manage the cluster hardware themselves. Whether Spark runs on a cloud service or a self-managed cluster, the code you write is identical. This portability is one of the reasons Spark became the industry standard: learn it once, use it everywhere.

A Spark application has two kinds of processes running on the cluster: one driver and one or more executors. The driver is the coordinator — it runs your Python code, builds the plan for how to process the data, and assigns work to the executors. The executors are the workers — they receive tasks from the driver, read their assigned data, apply the transformations, and return results. This two-role architecture is the foundation of everything Spark does. Every concept in this course — DataFrames, transformations, actions, shuffles, joins — operates within this driver-executor model. Understanding it now will make every later lesson clearer.
The NordGrid Operations Center

NordGrid’s operations center in Stavanger is a good analogy for Spark’s architecture. The shift supervisor sits at a central desk with a complete map of all four regions and a list of today’s work orders. The supervisor does not go into the field — they plan, coordinate, and monitor. When a new batch of work arrives (200 turbine inspections), the supervisor examines the list, groups inspections by region, and radios each regional crew with their assignments. The crews drive to their turbines, perform inspections, and radio back the results. The supervisor collects all results into a single report.

blocks.title: Spark’s driver-executor architecture
blocks.content:
┌───────────────────────┐
                     │       DRIVER          │
                     │  (Shift Supervisor)    │
                     │                        │
                     │  • Runs your code      │
                     │  • Builds the plan      │
                     │  • Assigns tasks         │
                     │  • Collects results      │
                     └───────────┬───────────┘
                                │
              ┌────────────┼────────────┐
              │              │              │
              ▼              ▼              ▼
   ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐
   │  EXECUTOR 1  │ │  EXECUTOR 2  │ │  EXECUTOR 3  │
   │ (Field Crew) │ │ (Field Crew) │ │ (Field Crew) │
   │             │ │             │ │             │
   │ • Read data  │ │ • Read data  │ │ • Read data  │
   │ • Transform  │ │ • Transform  │ │ • Transform  │
   │ • Return     │ │ • Return     │ │ • Return     │
   └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘
blocks.caption: The driver is the brain; executors are the hands. The driver never touches the data directly — it plans and coordinates. Executors never plan — they execute the tasks they receive.

blocks.content: What the Driver Does

The driver process runs on one machine in the cluster (or on your laptop in local mode). When you run a PySpark script, the driver is the process that starts. Your Python code executes on the driver: creating the SparkSession, defining DataFrames, chaining transformations, and calling actions. The driver does not process the actual data — it builds a plan. When you write df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0), the driver records that a filter should happen, but no data is filtered yet. When you call an action like df.show(), the driver looks at the entire plan, optimizes it, breaks it into tasks, and sends those tasks to executors.

The driver is also responsible for collecting results. When an action like count() finishes, each executor has a partial count (the count of rows in its portion of the data). The executors send these partial counts back to the driver, and the driver adds them up to produce the final answer. For actions like show(5), only the first executor to find 5 matching rows sends them back — the driver does not need results from all executors.
What Executors Do

Executors are worker processes, each running on a separate machine in the cluster (or in separate threads in local mode). Each executor has its own memory and CPU cores, and it processes only the data assigned to it. When the driver sends a task that says ‘read partition 7 of the meters file and filter rows where daily_kw > 0,’ the executor reads that specific partition from disk, applies the filter, and holds the surviving rows in its memory. The executor does not know about the other partitions or what the other executors are doing. It simply executes its assigned task and waits for the next one.

blocks.title: The key distinction
blocks.content: The driver is small and smart — it holds the plan, the metadata, and the control logic, but never the actual data (except for small results returned by actions). Executors are large and specialized — they hold the data and perform the heavy computation, but they do not make decisions about what to do next. This separation is what makes Spark scalable: adding more executors adds more processing power without making the driver more complex.
How They Communicate

The driver and executors communicate over the network. The driver sends task descriptions to executors (lightweight messages that say what to do, not the data itself). Executors send status updates and small results back to the driver. The actual data moves between executors during certain operations — for example, when a join requires rows from different executors to be matched together. This data movement between executors is called a shuffle, and it is one of the most important performance concepts in Spark. You will encounter it in detail when you learn about aggregations and joins in later modules.

For NordGrid’s daily pipeline, a typical Spark application has one driver and four to eight executors. The driver runs on a small machine (it does not need much memory since it only holds the plan, not the data). Each executor runs on a larger machine with enough memory to hold its share of the 1.5 GB daily file plus working space for transformations. The total cluster memory is the sum of all executor memories — enough to handle the entire dataset comfortably, with room to spare for temporary data during shuffles and joins.

blocks.title: Common confusion
blocks.content: When learning Spark, it is tempting to think of the driver as the machine that ‘runs the code.’ In one sense this is true — your Python code runs on the driver. But the data processing — the reading, filtering, joining, aggregating that takes time — runs on executors. The driver only orchestrates. This means that a bug in your transformation logic happens on executors, not on the driver, which is why Spark error messages can sometimes look unfamiliar.

For a cluster to process data in parallel, the data must be split into pieces that different executors can work on independently. Spark calls these pieces partitions. When Spark reads NordGrid’s 1.5 GB meter file, it does not hand the entire file to one executor. It divides the file into partitions — say, 12 partitions of roughly 125 MB each — and assigns each partition to an executor. Each executor processes its partitions independently, in parallel with the other executors. The number of partitions determines how many pieces of work exist, which in turn determines how much parallelism Spark can achieve.
The Pizza Analogy

Imagine a pizza that needs to be eaten by four people. If the pizza is left whole, only one person can eat at a time — the others wait. If you cut it into 4 slices, each person gets a slice and eats simultaneously. Cutting into 8 slices is even better: each person gets 2 slices, and if one person finishes their first slice early, they can start the second while others are still eating. But cutting the pizza into 1,000 slices creates overhead — each slice is tiny, and more time is spent passing slices around than eating. Partitions work the same way: too few means idle executors, too many means excessive coordination overhead, and the right number balances parallelism with efficiency.

blocks.title: Partition count and parallelism
blocks.content:
NordGrid’s 1.5 GB file with 4 executors:

2 partitions (under-partitioned):
  Executor 1: ██████████████ (750 MB)
  Executor 2: ██████████████ (750 MB)
  Executor 3: (idle)
  Executor 4: (idle)
  → Only 2 of 4 executors working. Half the cluster is wasted.

8 partitions (well-partitioned):
  Executor 1: ███████ ███████ (2 × ~190 MB)
  Executor 2: ███████ ███████ (2 × ~190 MB)
  Executor 3: ███████ ███████ (2 × ~190 MB)
  Executor 4: ███████ ███████ (2 × ~190 MB)
  → All 4 executors busy. If one finishes early, it picks up the next partition.

1000 partitions (over-partitioned):
  Each partition: ~1.5 MB (tiny)
  → Scheduling 1000 tasks creates overhead that exceeds the benefit.
blocks.caption: The ideal partition count gives every executor enough work to stay busy without creating so many tiny tasks that coordination overhead dominates.

blocks.content: How Spark Chooses Partition Count

When you read a file, Spark determines the initial partition count based on the file size and the file format. For a Parquet file, each Parquet row group typically becomes one partition. For a CSV file, Spark splits the file roughly by a configurable block size (default 128 MB), creating one partition per block. NordGrid’s 1.5 GB CSV file would produce about 12 partitions at the default block size. You do not usually need to control this number when reading files — Spark’s defaults are reasonable for most cases.

For operations that rearrange data across executors (like groupBy or join), Spark uses a separate partition count controlled by the configuration property spark.sql.shuffle.partitions. The default value is 200, which works well for medium to large datasets. For NordGrid’s daily 1.5 GB file, 200 shuffle partitions might be more than necessary — each partition would hold only about 7.5 MB — but the overhead is minimal. You will learn how to tune this value in Module 2 when you study configuration, and again in Module 6 when you see how shuffles actually work.
Partitions and Parallelism

Partitions determine the upper limit of parallelism. If your data has 8 partitions and your cluster has 4 executors with 2 cores each (8 cores total), then all 8 partitions can be processed simultaneously — one per core. If your data has 4 partitions but you have 8 cores, then only 4 cores are active and 4 sit idle. Conversely, if your data has 16 partitions and you have 8 cores, Spark processes the first 8 partitions in parallel, then the next 8. The job takes roughly twice as long as it would with 8 partitions, but all cores stay busy throughout.

blocks.title: Mental model
blocks.content: Think of partitions as tasks on a to-do list and executor cores as workers. Each worker picks up one task at a time. More tasks than workers means the workers stay continuously busy. Fewer tasks than workers means some workers are idle. Far more tasks than workers means excessive context-switching. The sweet spot is 2–4 partitions per core, giving every core enough work without excessive overhead.
What Lives Inside a Partition

Each partition holds a subset of the rows from your DataFrame. When Spark reads NordGrid’s meter file into 12 partitions, partition 1 might hold rows 1 through 42,000, partition 2 holds rows 42,001 through 84,000, and so on. The rows are not shuffled or rearranged — they appear in the partition in the same order they appeared in the source file. Every column is present in every partition; partitioning splits rows, not columns. When you apply a filter like df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0), each executor applies the filter to its own partitions independently, keeping the surviving rows and discarding the rest.

This independence is crucial. Because each partition is self-contained, executors do not need to communicate with each other during a filter operation. Executor 1 filters its partitions at the same time Executor 2 filters its partitions — neither depends on the other’s results. Operations that work partition-by-partition without cross-communication are called narrow transformations, and they are the fastest operations in Spark. Operations that require data to move between executors (like groupBy or join) are called wide transformations, and they involve a shuffle. You will learn this distinction in detail in Module 6.

blocks.title: For later
blocks.content: You do not need to memorize partition counts or tuning strategies right now. The important concept from this lesson is what partitions are (pieces of data distributed across executors) and why they exist (to enable parallelism). The details of partition tuning become relevant when you learn about shuffles and performance in later modules.

Every lesson in this course uses the same fictional company to illustrate PySpark concepts: NordGrid Energy, a Northern European grid operator that manages renewable energy infrastructure across four geographic regions. NordGrid is not a toy example — it is modeled on the data realities of real European energy operators, scaled down to a size that is large enough to be realistic but small enough to understand completely. The company, its data, and its pipeline architecture will be your constant reference point throughout every module. This lesson introduces the scenario in full so that when later lessons say ‘NordGrid’s daily meter readings,’ you know exactly what that means.
The Company

NordGrid Energy operates wind farms and solar installations across Northern Europe. The company is part of a larger parent organization called GridUnion Continental, but at the junior level, you only need to know about NordGrid itself. NordGrid has one data engineering team responsible for building and maintaining the data pipelines that process meter readings, turbine telemetry, and weather forecasts. This team writes PySpark code daily, and their codebase is the basis for the examples in this course.
The Four Regions

NordGrid divides its operations into four geographic regions: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, and WEST. Each region has its own wind farms, solar installations, substations, and smart meters. The region code is one of the most common columns in NordGrid’s data — it appears in meter readings, turbine reports, and substation records. When you see the column name region in a code example, it always contains one of these four values. The four-region structure keeps examples simple while giving you enough variety to practice grouping, filtering, and joining by region.

blocks.title: NordGrid’s four-region operating structure
blocks.caption: NordGrid’s 500,000 meters and 2,000 turbines are distributed across four regions. The NORTH region is the largest — a pattern that will become relevant when you learn about data skew in the mid-level track.

blocks.content: The Data Sources

NordGrid’s data engineering team works with four primary data sources. Each source has a consistent schema (column names and types) that remains the same throughout this course. You will see these columns hundreds of times in code examples, so it is worth becoming familiar with them now. The meter readings are the most commonly used dataset — they appear in nearly every lesson from Module 3 onward.

rows: Daily energy consumption from 500,000 smart meters
Hourly sensor data from 2,000 wind turbines
Temperature and forecast from external weather API
Static reference table linking meters to substations

blocks.content: The Consumer Teams

At the junior level, NordGrid has one data engineering team that builds and maintains all pipelines. The team is small — a handful of engineers who share responsibility for the Bronze ingestion scripts, the Silver transformation pipelines, and the Gold aggregation jobs. In the mid-level and senior-level tracks, NordGrid’s organization grows: multiple teams, cross-functional consumers, and governance challenges emerge. For now, the single-team structure keeps things simple — you can focus on the PySpark code without worrying about organizational complexity.
The Pipeline Architecture

NordGrid’s data flows through a three-layer architecture that you will learn about in detail in the next lesson. Raw data arrives from meters, turbines, and weather APIs in various formats (CSV, JSON, Parquet). The data engineering team’s PySpark pipelines read this raw data, clean it, validate it, join it with reference tables, and produce analytical summaries. The final outputs feed dashboards for the operations team, billing calculations for the finance team, and regulatory reports for compliance. Every stage of this pipeline uses PySpark, and every module in this course teaches a technique that appears somewhere in this pipeline.

blocks.title: Why a consistent scenario matters
blocks.content: Using the same company, the same column names, and the same pipeline throughout the course means you build familiarity over time. By Module 5, you will not need to read the column descriptions — you will know that meter_id identifies a meter, daily_kw is the daily energy reading in kilowatts, and region is one of NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, or WEST. This familiarity frees your mental energy to focus on the new PySpark concept each lesson teaches, rather than constantly adapting to unfamiliar data.

NordGrid’s data does not go directly from a smart meter to a dashboard. Raw meter readings arrive as messy CSV files: columns have inconsistent types, some rows contain negative energy values from malfunctioning sensors, and date formats vary between regions. Before any analyst or dashboard can use this data, it must be cleaned, validated, and transformed. NordGrid organizes this work into three layers — Bronze, Silver, and Gold — where each layer has a specific job and a clear contract about what the data looks like. This pattern is called the medallion architecture, and it is used by data engineering teams across the industry.
Bronze — Raw Ingestion

The Bronze layer is the landing zone for raw data. It stores data exactly as it arrived from the source, with no transformations, no cleaning, and no filtering. NordGrid’s Bronze layer receives the daily meter CSV file, the hourly SCADA Parquet files, and the weather API JSON responses. The only operation performed in the Bronze layer is copying: the raw file is written to storage as-is. If a meter exports a negative daily_kw value because of a sensor malfunction, that negative value appears in Bronze. If a CSV column contains a date formatted as ‘03-22-2026” instead of the standard “2026-03-22,” Bronze stores it unchanged.

The purpose of keeping raw data untouched is debuggability. When something goes wrong in a downstream transformation — a dashboard shows unexpected values, or a billing calculation is off — the team can always go back to Bronze and look at exactly what arrived from the source. If Bronze had already been cleaned, the original evidence would be lost. Bronze is the audit trail, the source of truth, the ‘before’ picture that makes every ‘after’ picture verifiable.

blocks.title: The medallion architecture — Bronze, Silver, Gold
blocks.content:
Raw Sources                 Bronze              Silver              Gold
┌────────────┐          ┌───────────┐    ┌───────────┐    ┌───────────┐
│ Meter CSV  │  ───▶  │ Raw CSV   │    │ Validated │    │ Regional  │
│ SCADA files│  copy   │ Raw Parq  │ ─▶ │ Typed     │ ─▶ │ summaries │
│ Weather API│  as-is  │ Raw JSON  │    │ Joined    │    │ Billing   │
└────────────┘          └───────────┘    └───────────┘    └───────────┘
                        No changes      PySpark          PySpark
                        No cleaning     cleans,          aggregates,
                        Exact copy      validates,       pivots,
                                        casts types,     computes KPIs
                                        joins reference

                        Debuggable      Reliable         Business-ready
blocks.caption: Each layer has a clear purpose. Bronze preserves the raw evidence. Silver produces reliable, typed, validated data. Gold produces the aggregated outputs that business consumers need.

blocks.content: Silver — Cleaned and Validated

The Silver layer is where PySpark does the heavy lifting. A PySpark pipeline reads from Bronze, applies a series of transformations, and writes the results to Silver. These transformations include type casting (converting the daily_kw column from a string to a proper numeric type), filtering (removing rows with negative energy values or null meter IDs), date standardization (converting all date formats to the ISO standard YYYY-MM-DD), and joining with reference tables (adding the substation name and capacity from the substation reference table). The output is a clean, typed, validated Parquet file that any downstream consumer can trust.

The contract between Silver and its consumers is strict: every column has a defined type, no invalid values remain, reference data has been joined, and the data is stored in Parquet format for fast reading. If the Bronze CSV has a date column stored as strings, Silver has that column stored as a proper DateType. If Bronze has rows with null meter IDs (a data quality issue), Silver has removed them. This contract means that anyone reading from Silver — an analyst, a dashboard, a machine learning model — does not need to worry about data quality. The Silver pipeline has already handled it.
Gold — Business-Ready Aggregates

The Gold layer contains aggregated, summarized, and shaped data designed for specific business use cases. While Silver contains individual meter readings (one row per meter per day), Gold contains summaries: total daily energy per region, monthly billing aggregates per customer type, or weekly anomaly counts for the operations dashboard. Each Gold table is built by a PySpark pipeline that reads from Silver, groups and aggregates the data, and writes the result. Gold tables are typically much smaller than Silver — NordGrid’s Silver meter table has 500,000 rows per day, while the Gold regional summary has only 4 rows per day (one per region).

The distinction between Silver and Gold is not about quality — both are high quality. The distinction is about granularity and purpose. Silver is granular (one row per meter per day) and general-purpose (many teams read it). Gold is aggregated (one row per region per day, or one row per customer type per month) and specific to a use case. A single Silver table might feed five different Gold tables, each serving a different business need.

rows: Raw data, exact copy from source
Unknown — may have nulls, bad types, duplicates
High — strict schema, no invalid values

blocks.content: The three-layer pattern creates a clean separation of concerns. The Bronze pipeline worries only about ingestion — getting the raw data safely into storage. The Silver pipeline worries about data quality — cleaning, validating, and enriching. The Gold pipeline worries about business logic — computing the specific aggregations each consumer needs. If a data quality rule changes (for example, NordGrid decides that readings above 10,000 kW are also invalid), only the Silver pipeline changes. Bronze and Gold are unaffected. This modularity makes the system easier to maintain, debug, and evolve as business requirements change.

blocks.title: Where PySpark fits in the layers
blocks.content: Almost all of the PySpark techniques you will learn in this course — reading files, filtering, transforming columns, aggregating, joining, writing Parquet — are used in the Silver and Gold pipelines. Bronze is simple (just copy the file). The real data engineering work happens in the Silver and Gold transformations, and that is where PySpark shines.

Apache Spark is the distributed processing engine. PySpark is the Python interface to that engine. When you write PySpark code, you are writing Python — the same language you already know, with the same syntax, the same variables, the same functions. But instead of processing data on your laptop’s single CPU, your PySpark code tells Spark to process the data across every machine in the cluster. This lesson explains how PySpark relates to Spark, why Python became the dominant language for Spark development, and what happens behind the scenes when your Python code becomes distributed execution.
Spark’s Multiple APIs

Spark is written in Scala, a language that runs on the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). The original Spark API was Scala-only: you wrote your transformations in Scala, and Spark executed them on the JVM across the cluster. As Spark grew in popularity, the team added APIs in other languages: Java (also JVM-based), Python (PySpark), and R (SparkR). All four APIs provide access to the same Spark engine. The same operations — filter, groupBy, join, write — are available in every language. The execution plans are identical. A pipeline written in PySpark produces the same results as the same pipeline written in Scala.
Why Python Won

PySpark accounts for roughly 70% of Spark usage in the industry. The reason is the Python ecosystem. Data engineers already use Python for scripting, automation, and orchestration. Data scientists use Python for Pandas, scikit-learn, and TensorFlow. Analysts use Python for Jupyter notebooks and visualization. When an organization adopts Spark, choosing PySpark means the existing Python team can use it immediately without learning a new language. The alternative — training an entire team on Scala — is expensive and slow. Python’s readability also matters: PySpark code is easier to review, debug, and maintain than equivalent Scala code for most teams.
What Happens When You Run PySpark Code

When you write df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0) in PySpark, your Python code does not actually filter any rows. It calls a Python method that sends a message to the JVM (the Java Virtual Machine where Spark’s core engine runs), telling it to add a filter node to the execution plan. The plan is built and optimized entirely inside the JVM. When you call an action like show(), the JVM-based engine distributes the optimized plan to executors, which execute it using Spark’s compiled Scala code. The actual data processing — the reading, filtering, transforming, and writing — happens in the JVM, not in Python.

blocks.title: PySpark code flow — from Python to distributed execution
blocks.content:
Your Python Code          PySpark Library           Spark JVM Engine
┌──────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐
│ df.filter(...)   │ ─▶ │ Translates to    │ ─▶ │ Adds filter to   │
│ df.groupBy(...)  │    │ JVM commands     │    │ execution plan   │
│ df.show()        │    │ via Py4J bridge  │    │ Optimizes plan   │
└──────────────────┘    └──────────────────┘    │ Distributes to   │
                                               │ executors        │
   Python runs                                 └────────┬─────────┘
   on the driver                                        │
   only                                     ┌─────────┼────────┐
                                             ▼          ▼          ▼
                                          Executor  Executor  Executor
                                          (JVM)     (JVM)     (JVM)
                                          Processes data using Spark’s
                                          compiled engine — not Python
blocks.caption: PySpark is a thin Python wrapper. Your code runs on the driver in Python, but the heavy data processing runs on executors inside the JVM. This is why PySpark is nearly as fast as Scala Spark for DataFrame operations.

blocks.content: The Py4J Bridge

The connection between Python and the JVM is managed by a library called Py4J. When your PySpark code calls df.filter(...), Py4J translates that Python method call into a Java method call on the Spark engine running inside the JVM. This happens transparently — you never interact with Py4J directly, and you do not need to know Java. The bridge is fast for plan-building operations (which are just method calls) but becomes a bottleneck if large amounts of data need to cross the boundary, which is why User-Defined Functions are slower than built-in operations.

This architecture has an important performance implication. Because the actual data processing runs inside the JVM, PySpark DataFrame operations are nearly as fast as equivalent Scala operations. The Python layer adds a small amount of overhead on the driver (milliseconds per command), but the driver is just building a plan — the execution time is determined by the JVM engine on the executors. For NordGrid’s pipelines, the difference between PySpark and Scala Spark is typically less than 5%, which is negligible compared to the productivity advantage of using Python.

blocks.title: When Python does slow things down
blocks.content: The near-zero performance gap applies to DataFrame operations (filter, groupBy, join, and so on) because those run in the JVM. If you write a User-Defined Function (UDF) in Python, Spark must serialize data from the JVM to Python and back for every row — this is dramatically slower. You will learn about UDFs and their performance implications in Module 9. For now, the rule is simple: use PySpark’s built-in DataFrame operations, and your code will be fast.

Throughout this course, every code example is written in PySpark. The concepts — DataFrames, lazy evaluation, transformations, actions, shuffles — are Spark concepts that exist in every API. If you ever need to read Scala Spark code (in a colleague’s repository, in a Stack Overflow answer, or in Spark’s own documentation), you will find that the operations look almost identical. The syntax differs slightly, but the logic is the same. Learning PySpark teaches you Spark.

sections.title: The Life of a Spark Job

blocks.content: When you write a PySpark pipeline and call an action like write.parquet(), a complex sequence of events unfolds inside the Spark engine. The driver builds an execution plan, the optimizer rewrites that plan to be more efficient, the plan is broken into stages and tasks, the tasks are sent to executors, the executors process data in parallel, and the results are written to disk. Understanding this sequence — even at a high level — gives you the vocabulary to read the Spark UI, interpret error messages, and reason about why a pipeline takes the time it does. This lesson traces a NordGrid pipeline through every phase of execution.
The Pipeline

Consider NordGrid’s daily Silver pipeline: it reads the raw meter CSV from Bronze, filters out invalid readings (null meter IDs and negative kW values), casts the daily_kw column from string to numeric, joins with the substation reference table, and writes the result to Silver as a Parquet file. This pipeline involves five transformations and one action (the write). Let us trace what happens when the script runs.

# NordGrid's daily Silver pipeline — a simplified version
# This code runs on the DRIVER (your Python process)

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

# Step 1: Read raw meter CSV from Bronze
meters = spark.read.csv('bronze/meters/2026-03-22.csv', header=True)

# Step 2: Remove invalid readings
meters = meters.filter(col('meter_id').isNotNull())
meters = meters.filter(col('daily_kw').cast('double') > 0)

# Step 3: Cast types
meters = meters.withColumn('daily_kw', col('daily_kw').cast('double'))

# Step 4: Join with substation reference
substations = spark.read.csv('bronze/substations.csv', header=True)
meters = meters.join(substations, 'substation_id', 'left')

# Step 5: Write to Silver — THIS is the action that triggers execution
meters.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('silver/meters/2026-03-22/')
Phase 1 — Plan Building

When each transformation line executes, the driver records it as a node in an internal plan graph. No data is read, no files are opened, no rows are filtered. The driver is simply assembling a description of what should happen. By the time the write line is reached, the plan contains all five transformations and the two data sources. The plan is a lightweight data structure in the driver’s memory — it describes the pipeline’s intent without executing it. This is lazy evaluation, which you will study in depth in Module 3.
Phase 2 — Optimization

When the write action triggers execution, the Spark engine’s optimizer — called Catalyst — examines the entire plan before running anything. It looks for opportunities to make the plan faster. For NordGrid’s pipeline, the optimizer might combine the two filter conditions into a single filter, push the column type cast earlier so it happens during the file read, and determine that the substation reference table is small enough to broadcast to all executors instead of shuffling the larger meters table. These optimizations happen automatically and are often more effective than what a human would write manually.
Phase 3 — Stages and Tasks

The optimized plan is divided into stages. A stage is a group of transformations that can execute without moving data between executors. The NordGrid pipeline has at least two stages: one for reading and filtering the meters CSV (each executor reads its own partitions), and one for the join with substations (which requires data to move between executors so matching rows end up together). The boundary between stages is always a shuffle — an operation that requires data redistribution.

Each stage is further divided into tasks. A task is the smallest unit of work: one transformation applied to one partition of data. If the meters file is split into 12 partitions, the first stage has 12 tasks — one per partition. Each task reads its partition from disk, applies the filters, and casts the types. Spark sends these 12 tasks to whatever executors have available cores. If there are 4 executors with 2 cores each (8 cores), 8 tasks run in parallel, then the remaining 4 run as cores become free.

blocks.title: From plan to stages to tasks
blocks.content:
Plan (what you wrote):         Stages (how Spark divides it):    Tasks (parallel units):

  Read CSV                     Stage 1: Read + Filter            12 tasks (one per partition)
     │                           (no data movement needed)       ──▶ Runs on 8 cores
  Filter nulls                    │                                  in parallel
     │                           │
  Filter kw > 0                   ▼
     │                         Shuffle (data redistribution)
  Cast types                      │
     │                           ▼
  Join substations             Stage 2: Join + Write             200 tasks (shuffle partitions)
     │                           (after shuffle, matching        ──▶ Runs in waves
  Write Parquet                    rows are together)                 across 8 cores
blocks.caption: The plan becomes stages at shuffle boundaries. Each stage becomes parallel tasks — one per partition. Tasks are the units that executors actually process.

blocks.content: Phase 4 — Execution and Results

The driver sends tasks to executors, which process them and report completion. As each executor finishes a task, the driver sends it the next available task. When all tasks in stage 1 are complete, the shuffle moves data between executors to prepare for stage 2 (the join). Then stage 2’s tasks execute: each executor joins its portion of the meter data with the substation reference and writes the result to a Parquet file. When all stage 2 tasks are complete, the write action is finished, and the driver reports success.

blocks.title: The vocabulary
blocks.content: A job is triggered by one action (the write). A job contains one or more stages. A stage contains one or more tasks. These are the terms you will see in the Spark UI, and they are the terms your colleagues use when discussing pipeline performance. ‘The join stage has 200 tasks and the slowest task took 3 minutes’ — this sentence uses all three terms and tells an experienced engineer exactly where to look for the bottleneck.

The Spark UI at localhost:4040 (which you will explore in Module 2) shows this entire sequence visually. You can see the job, click into its stages, see the task count and duration for each stage, and identify which stage is the slowest. For NordGrid’s daily pipeline, the join stage is usually the bottleneck because the shuffle moves data across the network. The read-and-filter stage is fast because each executor reads its own data independently. This kind of reasoning — understanding why one stage is slow by understanding what it does — is the foundation of Spark performance tuning.

The previous nine lessons introduced the core concepts: the single-machine bottleneck, the cluster idea, Apache Spark as the processing engine, the driver-executor architecture, partitions, the NordGrid scenario, the Bronze-Silver-Gold pipeline, PySpark as the Python API, and the lifecycle of a Spark job. This lesson takes a step back and looks at the physical cluster itself — the actual machines, the resource manager that allocates them, and the lifecycle of an application from submission to completion. This is the operational layer that sits beneath everything you have learned so far.
The Cluster Manager

A Spark cluster does not manage itself. A separate piece of software called the cluster manager handles the physical resources: which machines are available, how much memory each has, and which Spark applications are currently running. When the driver starts and requests 4 executors with 8 GB of memory each, it sends that request to the cluster manager. The cluster manager finds 4 machines (or 4 slots on machines) with enough free memory, starts executor processes on them, and tells the driver where the executors are running. From that point on, the driver communicates directly with the executors.

There are three common cluster managers. YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) is the cluster manager built into Hadoop ecosystems and used by many on-premises and cloud deployments. Kubernetes is the container orchestration platform increasingly used for Spark in cloud-native environments. Spark also has its own built-in standalone cluster manager for simpler deployments. NordGrid uses YARN on their managed cloud service. You do not need to know the details of any cluster manager at this stage — the important concept is that a separate system handles resource allocation so that Spark can focus on data processing.

blocks.title: Where the cluster manager fits
blocks.content:
Your PySpark Script
        │
        ▼
   ┌────────────────┐
   │    DRIVER       │
   │ (plans work)    │ ─── requests 4 executors ───┐
   └────────┬───────┘                                  │
            │                                         ▼
            │                                  ┌─────────────────┐
            │                                  │ CLUSTER MANAGER  │
            │                                  │ (YARN / K8s)     │
            │                                  │ Allocates        │
            │                                  │ machines         │
            │                                  └─────┬───────────┘
            │                                        │
            │       starts executors on available machines
            │                                        │
            ▼                                        ▼
     ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐
     │Executor 1│  │Executor 2│  │Executor 3│  │Executor 4│
     └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘
blocks.caption: The cluster manager is the gatekeeper that turns a pool of physical machines into allocated resources for Spark applications. The driver requests resources; the cluster manager provides them.

blocks.content: Local Mode — One Machine Pretending to Be Many

When you are learning PySpark or developing a new pipeline, you typically run Spark in local mode on your laptop. In local mode, the driver and all executors run as threads within a single process on one machine. There is no cluster, no network communication, and no cluster manager. Spark simulates the distributed behavior: data is still split into partitions, tasks are still created, and the execution plan is still optimized. The only difference is that everything happens in one process on one machine.

Local mode is valuable because it lets you test and debug PySpark code without setting up a cluster. NordGrid’s data engineers write and test their pipelines on a small sample of data in local mode, then deploy the same code to the production cluster without changes. The PySpark code is identical in both environments — only the Spark configuration changes (from local mode to cluster mode). This portability is one of Spark’s most practical advantages: develop locally, deploy to the cluster, and the behavior is the same.
Local Mode Limitations

Local mode has one important limitation: it cannot simulate the network communication between machines. Operations that involve shuffling data between executors (like joins and aggregations) behave identically in terms of logic, but their performance characteristics in local mode do not reflect what happens on a real cluster. A join that takes 2 seconds locally might take 30 seconds on the cluster because the shuffle moves data over the network. NordGrid’s team uses local mode for correctness testing and the staging cluster for performance testing.
Driver vs Executor Responsibilities

rows: Yes — each executor holds its partitions
Yes — this is where the heavy computation happens
No — they are the allocated resources
Yes — sends status back to driver

blocks.content: The Complete Picture

You now have all the foundational pieces. Data grows beyond what one machine can handle (lesson 1). The solution is to split work across a cluster (lesson 2). Spark is the engine that manages this distribution (lesson 3). It uses a driver to plan and executors to process (lesson 4). Data is split into partitions so executors can work in parallel (lesson 5). NordGrid’s data flows through Bronze, Silver, and Gold layers (lessons 6 and 7). PySpark is the Python API that lets you write the transformations (lesson 8). A Spark job goes through plan building, optimization, staging, and execution (lesson 9). And the cluster manager provides the physical resources that make it all possible (this lesson).

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 2, you will create your first SparkSession — the object that connects your Python code to the Spark engine. Every concept from this module will become concrete: you will see the driver and executors in the Spark UI, configure memory and parallelism, and run your first PySpark commands. The foundation is set. Now it is time to build on it.
